title: Die drei Spinnerinnen
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 14. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The fourteenth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 14), a wry story about getting out of work. A lazy girl who will not spin is taken to the castle on her mother’s false boast that she loves nothing better; three strangely deformed women — one with a splay-foot, one with a drooping lip, one with a broad thumb — secretly spin her mountains of flax in return for being owned as her cousins at the wedding, and when the bridegroom learns that spinning is what disfigured them, he swears his bride shall never touch a spinning-wheel again. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, gieng, anfieng, fieng, erschrack, wußte, mußt, Noth, Hülfe, gethan, rathen and unverdroßner.

sentences.t: Es war ein Mädchen faul und wollte nicht spinnen, und die Mutter mochte sagen was sie wollte, sie konnte es nicht dazu bringen.
sentences.l: There was a girl lazy and would not spin, and the mother might say what she would, she could not bring it to it.
sentences.i: There was once a girl who was lazy and would not spin, and say what the mother might, she could not bring her to it.
sentences.n: “mochte sagen was sie wollte” is a set concessive frame — ‘might say what she would,’ i.e. no matter what she said. The girl is “es” (das Mädchen is neuter), so grammatically ‘it’ throughout — read it as ‘she.’

sentences.t: Endlich übernahm die Mutter einmal Zorn und Ungeduld, daß sie ihm Schläge gab, worüber es laut zu weinen anfieng.
sentences.l: At last wrath and impatience once overcame the mother, so that she gave it blows, whereupon it began loudly to weep.
sentences.i: At last anger and impatience got the better of the mother, so that she struck the girl, whereupon she began to cry aloud.
sentences.n: “übernahm … Zorn und Ungeduld” — here übernehmen is used impersonally, ‘anger and impatience overcame her.’ “ihm” and “es” both refer to das Mädchen (neuter): ‘to her,’ ‘she.’ “anfieng” is the 1857 spelling of anfing (anfangen, to begin), with the old -ie-.

sentences.t: Nun fuhr gerade die Königin vorbei, und als sie das Weinen hörte, ließ sie anhalten, trat in das Haus und fragte die Mutter, warum sie ihre Tochter schlüge, daß man draußen auf der Straße das Schreien hörte.
sentences.l: Now just then the queen drove past, and when she heard the weeping, she had [them] stop, stepped into the house and asked the mother why she was beating her daughter, so that one heard the screaming outside on the street.
sentences.i: Just then the queen happened to drive by, and when she heard the weeping, she had the carriage stop, stepped into the house, and asked the mother why she was beating her daughter so that the screaming could be heard out in the street.
sentences.n: “ließ … anhalten” — lassen + infinitive, ‘she had [it] stopped’ (the carriage). “schlüge” is subjunctive II of schlagen in the indirect question (‘why she was beating’) — reported speech takes the subjunctive, everywhere in Grimm.

sentences.t: Da schämte sich die Frau daß sie die Faulheit ihrer Tochter offenbaren sollte und sprach „ich kann sie nicht vom Spinnen abbringen, sie will immer und ewig spinnen, und ich bin arm und kann den Flachs nicht herbeischaffen.“
sentences.l: Then the woman was ashamed that she should reveal the laziness of her daughter and spoke, “I cannot get her away from the spinning, she wants always and forever to spin, and I am poor and cannot procure the flax.”
sentences.i: Then the woman was ashamed to have to reveal her daughter’s laziness, and said, “I cannot get her to stop spinning; she wants to spin for ever and ever, and I am poor and cannot get hold of the flax.”
sentences.n: The mother inverts the truth: the girl won’t spin, but shame makes her claim the opposite. “vom Spinnen abbringen” = to divert her from spinning; “abbringen” is separable (bringt ab). “herbeischaffen” (to procure, bring in) is likewise separable.

sentences.t: Da antwortete die Königin „ich höre nichts lieber als spinnen, und bin nicht vergnügter als wenn die Räder schnurren: gebt mir eure Tochter mit ins Schloß, ich habe Flachs genug, da soll sie spinnen so viel sie Lust hat.“
sentences.l: Then answered the queen, “I hear nothing more gladly than spinning, and am not more content than when the wheels whir: give me your daughter along into the castle, I have flax enough, there shall she spin as much as she has a mind.”
sentences.i: Then the queen answered, “There is nothing I would rather hear than spinning, and I am never happier than when the wheels are whirring: give me your daughter to take to the castle; I have flax enough, and there she shall spin as much as she likes.”
sentences.n: “nichts lieber … als” = ‘nothing more gladly than.’ “die Räder” are the spinning-wheels (Rad). “gebt … mit” is the respectful/plural imperative to the mother (mitgeben, separable). “so viel sie Lust hat” = as much as she pleases.

sentences.t: Die Mutter wars von Herzen gerne zufrieden und die Königin nahm das Mädchen mit.
sentences.l: The mother was heartily glad content with it, and the queen took the girl along.
sentences.i: The mother was heartily glad to agree, and the queen took the girl with her.
sentences.n: “wars” = war es (‘was it,’ contracted). “von Herzen gerne zufrieden” piles up ‘from the heart / gladly / content’ — an emphatic ‘only too glad.’ “nahm … mit” (mitnehmen, to take along) is separable.

sentences.t: Als sie ins Schloß gekommen waren, führte sie es hinauf zu drei Kammern, die lagen von unten bis oben voll vom schönsten Flachs.
sentences.l: When they had come into the castle, she led it up to three chambers, which lay from bottom to top full of the finest flax.
sentences.i: When they had come to the castle, she led her up to three chambers, which were full of the finest flax from bottom to top.
sentences.n: “es” is the girl (das Mädchen). “die lagen … voll” — the demonstrative die resumes the chambers, and ‘lay full’ is the idiom for ‘were packed full.’ “vom schönsten Flachs” = von dem schönsten Flachs.

sentences.t: „Nun spinn mir diesen Flachs,“ sprach sie, „und wenn du es fertig bringst, so sollst du meinen ältesten Sohn zum Gemahl haben; bist du gleich arm, so acht ich nicht darauf, dein unverdroßner Fleiß ist Ausstattung genug.“
sentences.l: “Now spin me this flax,” she spoke, “and if you bring it to completion, then you shall have my eldest son for husband; are you never so poor, I heed it not, your untiring diligence is dowry enough.”
sentences.i: “Now spin me this flax,” she said, “and if you get it done, you shall have my eldest son for a husband; poor though you are, I pay no heed to that — your untiring diligence is dowry enough.”
sentences.n: “bist du gleich arm” is a concessive with the verb first, ‘though you are poor’ (gleich here = ‘even though’). “acht ich nicht darauf” = ich achte nicht darauf, ‘I pay it no heed.’ “unverdroßner” is the 1857 spelling of unverdrossener (untiring).

sentences.t: Das Mädchen erschrack innerlich, denn es konnte den Flachs nicht spinnen, und wärs dreihundert Jahr alt geworden, und hätte jeden Tag vom Morgen bis Abend dabei gesessen.
sentences.l: The girl took fright inwardly, for it could not spin the flax, and had it grown three hundred years old, and had it sat at it every day from morning till evening.
sentences.i: The girl was inwardly terrified, for she could not spin the flax — not if she had lived to be three hundred years old and sat at it every day from morning till night.
sentences.n: “erschrack” is the 1857/archaic preterite of erschrecken (modern erschrak). “und wärs … geworden, und hätte … gesessen” is a subjunctive-II counterfactual, ‘even if she had grown … and had sat …’ — ‘wärs’ = wäre es. “dreihundert Jahr” keeps the old uninflected singular Jahr after a number.

sentences.t: Als es nun allein war, fieng es an zu weinen und saß so drei Tage ohne die Hand zu rühren.
sentences.l: When it now was alone, it began to weep and sat thus three days without stirring a hand.
sentences.i: Now when she was alone, she began to cry and sat like that for three days without lifting a hand.
sentences.n: “fieng … an” (anfangen, to begin) is separable and in the old -ie- spelling. “ohne die Hand zu rühren” = without stirring a hand; “rühren” = to stir, move.

sentences.t: Am dritten Tage kam die Königin und als sie sah daß noch nichts gesponnen war, verwunderte sie sich, aber das Mädchen entschuldigte sich damit, daß es vor großer Betrübnis über die Entfernung aus seiner Mutter Hause noch nicht hätte anfangen können.
sentences.l: On the third day the queen came, and when she saw that nothing yet was spun, she wondered, but the girl excused itself therewith, that out of great sorrow over the removal from its mother’s house it had not yet been able to begin.
sentences.i: On the third day the queen came, and when she saw that nothing had yet been spun, she was astonished; but the girl excused herself by saying that out of great sorrow at being taken from her mother’s house she had not yet been able to begin.
sentences.n: “verwunderte sich” (sich verwundern) = to be astonished. “entschuldigte sich damit, daß …” = excused herself on the grounds that … “Entfernung” here = the being removed/taken away. “hätte … anfangen können” is a subjunctive of reported speech — her excuse, not the narrator’s fact.

sentences.t: Das ließ sich die Königin gefallen, sagte aber beim Weggehen „morgen mußt du mir anfangen zu arbeiten.“
sentences.l: That the queen let herself please, but said on going away, “tomorrow you must begin to work for me.”
sentences.i: The queen let that pass, but said as she left, “Tomorrow you must set to work for me.”
sentences.n: “sich etwas gefallen lassen” = to put up with / accept something. “beim Weggehen” = on leaving (a nominalized verb). “mußt” keeps the 1857 ß (modern musst).

sentences.t: Als das Mädchen wieder allein war, wußte es sich nicht mehr zu rathen und zu helfen, und trat in seiner Betrübnis vor das Fenster.
sentences.l: When the girl was again alone, it no longer knew how to counsel and to help itself, and stepped in its sorrow before the window.
sentences.i: When the girl was alone again, she no longer knew what to do, and in her distress went to the window.
sentences.n: “sich nicht zu rathen und zu helfen wissen” is a fixed idiom, ‘to be at one’s wits’ end.’ “rathen” is the 1857 spelling of raten (to counsel). “seiner Betrübnis” — seiner because the girl is grammatically neuter (das Mädchen).

sentences.t: Da sah es drei Weiber herkommen, davon hatte die erste einen breiten Platschfuß, die zweite hatte eine so große Unterlippe, daß sie über das Kinn herunterhieng, und die dritte hatte einen breiten Daumen.
sentences.l: There it saw three women come along, of whom the first had a broad splay-foot, the second had so large a lower lip that it hung down over the chin, and the third had a broad thumb.
sentences.i: Then she saw three women coming along: the first had a broad, flat splay-foot, the second had a lower lip so big that it hung down over her chin, and the third had a broad thumb.
sentences.n: The three deformities are the whole point of the tale, each the mark of a spinner’s labour — the broad foot from working the treadle, the drooping lip from licking the thread, the broad thumb from twisting it (all named at the end). “herunterhieng” is the 1857 preterite of herunterhängen (modern hing herunter). “Platschfuß” = a broad, flat, splayed foot.

sentences.t: Die blieben vor dem Fenster stehen, schauten hinauf und fragten das Mädchen was ihm fehlte.
sentences.l: They remained standing before the window, looked up and asked the girl what was lacking to it.
sentences.i: They stopped before the window, looked up, and asked the girl what was wrong.
sentences.n: “blieben … stehen” (stehen bleiben, to stop, stand still) is separable. “was ihm fehlte” — fehlen takes the dative, ‘what was lacking to her’ = what was the matter (ihm = the neuter Mädchen).

sentences.t: Es klagte ihnen seine Noth, da trugen sie ihm ihre Hülfe an und sprachen „willst du uns zur Hochzeit einladen, dich unser nicht schämen und uns deine Basen heißen, auch an deinen Tisch setzen, so wollen wir dir den Flachs wegspinnen und das in kurzer Zeit.“
sentences.l: It lamented to them its distress; then they offered it their help and spoke, “if you will invite us to the wedding, not be ashamed of us and call us your cousins, and also seat us at your table, then we will spin the flax away for you, and that in a short time.”
sentences.i: She poured out her trouble to them, and they offered her their help, saying, “If you will invite us to the wedding, not be ashamed of us, call us your cousins, and seat us at your table too, then we will spin the flax away for you — and that in no time at all.”
sentences.n: “trugen … an” (antragen, to offer) is separable. “dich unser nicht schämen” — sich schämen takes the genitive (unser, ‘of us’). “uns deine Basen heißen” = to call us your cousins (heißen + double accusative). “Basen” (sg. Base) are female cousins/kinswomen; “Hülfe” is the 1857 spelling of Hilfe.

sentences.t: „Von Herzen gern,“ antwortete es, „kommt nur herein und fangt gleich die Arbeit an.“
sentences.l: “From the heart gladly,” it answered, “only come in and begin the work at once.”
sentences.i: “With all my heart,” she answered; “just come in and begin the work right away.”
sentences.n: “von Herzen gern” = most gladly, with all one’s heart. “kommt … herein” and “fangt … an” are plural imperatives (to the three women), both separable: hereinkommen and anfangen (fangt … an).

sentences.t: Da ließ es die drei seltsamen Weiber herein und machte in der ersten Kammer eine Lücke, wo sie sich hin setzten und ihr Spinnen anhuben.
sentences.l: Then it let the three strange women in and made in the first chamber a gap, where they sat themselves down and began their spinning.
sentences.i: Then she let the three strange women in and cleared a space in the first chamber, where they sat down and began their spinning.
sentences.n: “ließ … herein” (hereinlassen, to let in) is separable. “eine Lücke machen” = to clear a gap/space. “anhuben” is an archaic preterite of anheben (to begin), elevated for ‘fingen an’ — you will meet it again below.

sentences.t: Die eine zog den Faden und trat das Rad, die andere netzte den Faden, die dritte drehte ihn und schlug mit dem Finger auf den Tisch, und so oft sie schlug, fiel eine Zahl Garn zur Erde, und das war aufs feinste gesponnen.
sentences.l: The one drew the thread and trod the wheel, the other moistened the thread, the third twisted it and struck with the finger on the table, and as often as she struck, a batch of yarn fell to the earth, and that was spun in the finest way.
sentences.i: One drew out the thread and worked the treadle, another wetted the thread, the third twisted it and tapped her finger on the table; and each time she tapped, a skein of yarn fell to the floor, spun in the very finest way.
sentences.n: Here we watch each deformity being made: treading the wheel, moistening (licking) the thread, twisting it. “trat das Rad” = worked the treadle (treten, to tread). “netzte” = moistened (netzen). “eine Zahl Garn” = a counted-off portion/skein of yarn. “aufs feinste” = in the finest manner (aufs + superlative).

sentences.t: Vor der Königin verbarg sie die drei Spinnerinnen und zeigte ihr, so oft sie kam, die Menge des gesponnenen Garns, daß diese des Lobes kein Ende fand.
sentences.l: From the queen she hid the three spinners and showed her, as often as she came, the quantity of the spun yarn, so that this one found no end of praise.
sentences.i: From the queen she hid the three spinners, and each time the queen came, showed her the heap of spun yarn, so that the queen had no end of praise for it.
sentences.n: “verbarg” is the preterite of verbergen (to hide). “diese” (‘this one’) points to the queen, the nearer of the two women. “des Lobes kein Ende finden” = to find no end of praising — a genitive object (Lob → Lobes).

sentences.t: Als die erste Kammer leer war, giengs an die zweite, endlich an die dritte, und die war auch bald aufgeräumt.
sentences.l: When the first chamber was empty, it went at the second, at last at the third, and that was also soon cleared away.
sentences.i: When the first chamber was empty, they set to on the second, and finally the third, and that too was soon cleared out.
sentences.n: “giengs” = gieng es (‘it went,’ contracted), the old -ie- spelling of ging. “es geht an etwas” = one sets to / gets started on something. “aufgeräumt” = cleared, tidied (aufräumen).

sentences.t: Nun nahmen die drei Weiber Abschied und sagten zum Mädchen „vergiß nicht, was du uns versprochen hast, es wird dein Glück sein.“
sentences.l: Now the three women took leave and said to the girl, “forget not what you have promised us, it will be your fortune.”
sentences.i: Now the three women took their leave and said to the girl, “Do not forget what you have promised us — it will be the making of you.”
sentences.n: “Abschied nehmen” = to take one’s leave. “vergiß” is the familiar imperative of vergessen (to forget). “es wird dein Glück sein” = it will be your good fortune.

sentences.t: Als das Mädchen der Königin die leeren Kammern und den großen Haufen Garn zeigte, richtete sie die Hochzeit aus, und der Bräutigam freute sich daß er eine so geschickte und fleißige Frau bekäme und lobte sie gewaltig.
sentences.l: When the girl showed the queen the empty chambers and the great heap of yarn, she arranged the wedding, and the bridegroom rejoiced that he would get so skilful and diligent a wife, and praised her mightily.
sentences.i: When the girl showed the queen the empty chambers and the great heap of yarn, the queen arranged the wedding, and the bridegroom rejoiced that he was getting so deft and industrious a wife, and praised her mightily.
sentences.n: “richtete … aus” (ausrichten, to arrange, put on) is separable. “bekäme” is subjunctive II in reported thought — what the bridegroom believed he was getting. “geschickt” = skilful, deft; “fleißig” = diligent.

sentences.t: „Ich habe drei Basen,“ sprach das Mädchen, „und da sie mir viel Gutes gethan haben, so wollte ich sie nicht gern in meinem Glück vergessen: erlaubt doch daß ich sie zu der Hochzeit einlade und daß sie mit an dem Tisch sitzen.“
sentences.l: “I have three cousins,” spoke the girl, “and since they have done me much good, I would not gladly forget them in my good fortune: do allow that I invite them to the wedding and that they sit along at the table.”
sentences.i: “I have three cousins,” said the girl, “and since they have done me many kindnesses, I should not like to forget them now in my good fortune: do allow me to invite them to the wedding, and to have them sit with us at the table.”
sentences.n: “gethan” is the 1857 spelling of getan (done), from thun (modern tun). “erlaubt doch” — plural/respectful imperative softened by doch, ‘do please allow.’ “einlade” is the 1st-person of einladen (separable: lade ein).

sentences.t: Die Königin und der Bräutigam sprachen „warum sollen wir das nicht erlauben?“
sentences.l: The queen and the bridegroom spoke, “why should we not allow that?”
sentences.i: The queen and the bridegroom said, “Why should we not allow it?”

sentences.t: Als nun das Fest anhub, traten die drei Jungfern in wunderlicher Tracht herein, und die Braut sprach „seid willkommen, liebe Basen.“
sentences.l: When now the feast began, the three maidens stepped in in strange garb, and the bride spoke, “be welcome, dear cousins.”
sentences.i: Now when the feast began, the three maidens came in in strange attire, and the bride said, “Be welcome, dear cousins.”
sentences.n: “anhub” is an archaic preterite of anheben (to begin), a solemn word for a feast starting (cf. anhuben above). “wunderlicher Tracht” = strange, odd costume (Tracht = garb, attire). “traten … herein” (hereintreten) is separable.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sagte der Bräutigam, „wie kommst du zu der garstigen Freundschaft?“
sentences.l: “Ah,” said the bridegroom, “how do you come by the ugly kinfolk?”
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the bridegroom, “however did you come by such hideous kinfolk?”
sentences.n: “wie kommst du zu …” = how did you come by / get … “Freundschaft” here has its older sense of ‘kindred, relations,’ not ‘friendship’; “garstig” = ugly, nasty.

sentences.t: Darauf gieng er zu der einen mit dem breiten Platschfuß und fragte „wovon habt ihr einen solchen breiten Fuß?“
sentences.l: Thereupon he went to the one with the broad splay-foot and asked, “from what have you such a broad foot?”
sentences.i: Then he went up to the one with the broad splay-foot and asked, “What gave you such a broad foot?”
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging. “wovon” = ‘from what.’ He addresses her with the respectful plural “ihr / habt” (‘have you’).

sentences.t: „Vom Treten,“ antwortete sie, „vom Treten.“
sentences.l: “From the treading,” she answered, “from the treading.”
sentences.i: “From treading,” she answered, “from treading.”
sentences.n: The broad foot comes from working the spinning-wheel’s treadle (treten, to tread). The doubled answer gives each of the three women a little refrain.

sentences.t: Da gieng der Bräutigam zur zweiten und sprach „wovon habt ihr nur die herunterhängende Lippe?“
sentences.l: Then the bridegroom went to the second and spoke, “from what, pray, have you the hanging-down lip?”
sentences.i: Then the bridegroom went to the second and said, “And what gave you that drooping lip?”
sentences.n: “nur” here is a flavouring particle, ‘however, pray.’ “herunterhängende” is the present participle of herunterhängen used as an adjective, ‘hanging-down.’

sentences.t: „Vom Lecken,“ antwortete sie, „vom Lecken.“
sentences.l: “From the licking,” she answered, “from the licking.”
sentences.i: “From licking,” she answered, “from licking.”
sentences.n: The drooping lower lip comes from licking the flax-thread to moisten it while spinning (lecken, to lick).

sentences.t: Da fragte er die dritte „wovon habt ihr den breiten Daumen?“
sentences.l: Then he asked the third, “from what have you the broad thumb?”
sentences.i: Then he asked the third, “And what gave you that broad thumb?”

sentences.t: „Vom Faden drehen,“ antwortete sie, „vom Faden drehen.“
sentences.l: “From the thread-twisting,” she answered, “from the thread-twisting.”
sentences.i: “From twisting the thread,” she answered, “from twisting the thread.”
sentences.n: The broad thumb comes from twisting the thread (den Faden drehen). Each of the three tells now names the labour that deformed her — the very toil the girl was spared.

sentences.t: Da erschrack der Königssohn und sprach „so soll mir nun und nimmermehr meine schöne Braut ein Spinnrad anrühren.“
sentences.l: Then the king’s son took fright and spoke, “so shall my beautiful bride now and nevermore touch a spinning-wheel for me.”
sentences.i: Then the prince took fright and said, “Now and nevermore shall my beautiful bride so much as touch a spinning-wheel!”
sentences.n: “erschrack” — the 1857 preterite of erschrecken (modern erschrak). “nun und nimmermehr” = ‘now and nevermore,’ i.e. never again. “mir” is an ethical dative (‘as far as I am concerned’). “anrühren” (to touch) is separable. The spinners’ deformities have done their work — the prince frees the girl from spinning forever.

sentences.t: Damit war sie das böse Flachsspinnen los.
sentences.l: Therewith she was rid of the wretched flax-spinning.
sentences.i: And so she was rid of the hateful flax-spinning for good.
sentences.n: “etwas los sein” = to be rid of something. “böse” here = wretched, hateful. Note that only now, freed of spinning, is she plainly ‘sie’ (she) rather than the neuter ‘es’ of das Mädchen.
